In [24]:
# **Cell 1: Import Libraries**
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.api as sm
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import het_breuschpagan, acorr_breusch_godfrey
from statsmodels.stats.stattools import jarque_bera, durbin_watson
In [61]:
from plotnine.data import mtcars
# Create a copy
mtcars = mtcars.copy()
# Display the first few rows
mtcars.head()

name mpg cyl disp hp drat wt qsec vs am gear carb
0 Mazda RX4 21.0 6 160.0 110 3.90 2.620 16.46 0 1 4 4

In [62]:
# Import libraries
from plotnine.data import mtcars
from scipy.stats import pearsonr, spearmanr

# Load dataset
df = mtcars.copy()

# Select variables
x = df['wt']
y = df['mpg']

# Pearson correlation
pearson_corr, pearson_p = pearsonr(x, y)

# Spearman correlation
spearman_corr, spearman_p = spearmanr(x, y)

# Display results
print("="*50)
print("Pearson Correlation")
print("="*50)
print(f"Correlation coefficient (r): {pearson_corr:.4f}")
print(f"P-value: {pearson_p:.6f}")

print("\n" + "="*50)
print("Spearman Correlation")
print("="*50)
print(f"Correlation coefficient (ρ): {spearman_corr:.4f}")
print(f"P-value: {spearman_p:.6f}")

==================================================
Pearson Correlation
==================================================
Correlation coefficient (r): -0.8677
P-value: 0.000000

==================================================
Spearman Correlation
==================================================
Correlation coefficient (ρ): -0.8864
P-value: 0.000000
In [63]:
df = mtcars.copy()

# Pearson correlation
pearson_corr = df['wt'].corr(df['mpg'], method='pearson')

# Spearman correlation
spearman_corr = df['wt'].corr(df['mpg'], method='spearman')

print(f"Pearson correlation : {pearson_corr:.4f}")
print(f"Spearman correlation: {spearman_corr:.4f}")

Pearson correlation : -0.8677
Spearman correlation: -0.8864
In [64]:
# Import libraries
# Load dataset
df = mtcars.copy()

# Select variables
corr_data = df[['mpg', 'cyl', 'disp', 'hp', 'wt']]

# Pearson correlation matrix
corr_matrix = corr_data.corr(method='pearson')

# Display correlation matrix
print(corr_matrix.round(3))

        mpg    cyl   disp     hp     wt
mpg   1.000 -0.852 -0.848 -0.776 -0.868
cyl  -0.852  1.000  0.902  0.832  0.782
disp -0.848  0.902  1.000  0.791  0.888
hp   -0.776  0.832  0.791  1.000  0.659
wt   -0.868  0.782  0.888  0.659  1.000
In [65]:
# Display correlation matrix with 3 decimal places
corr_matrix.style.format("{:.3f}")
Out[65]:
  mpg cyl disp hp wt
mpg 1.000 -0.852 -0.848 -0.776 -0.868
cyl -0.852 1.000 0.902 0.832 0.782
disp -0.848 0.902 1.000 0.791 0.888
hp -0.776 0.832 0.791 1.000 0.659
wt -0.868 0.782 0.888 0.659 1.000
In [67]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

corr = corr_data.corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.imshow(corr, cmap='coolwarm', vmin=-1, vmax=1)

plt.xticks(range(len(corr)), corr.columns, rotation=45)
plt.yticks(range(len(corr)), corr.columns)

for i in range(len(corr)):
    for j in range(len(corr)):
        plt.text(j, i, f"{corr.iloc[i, j]:.2f}",
                 ha='center', va='center')

plt.colorbar()
plt.title("Pearson Correlation Matrix")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

LINEAR REGRESSION¶

Simple Linear Regression Using mtcars Data¶

In [68]:
# Import libraries
from plotnine.data import mtcars
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Load dataset
df = mtcars.copy()

# Define predictor and response
X = df['wt']
y = df['mpg']

# Add intercept (constant)
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Fit Simple Linear Regression model
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()

# Display regression results
print(model.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                    mpg   R-squared:                       0.753
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.745
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     91.38
Date:                Wed, 01 Jul 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.29e-10
Time:                        19:56:24   Log-Likelihood:                -80.015
No. Observations:                  32   AIC:                             164.0
Df Residuals:                      30   BIC:                             167.0
Df Model:                           1                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const         37.2851      1.878     19.858      0.000      33.450      41.120
wt            -5.3445      0.559     -9.559      0.000      -6.486      -4.203
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                        2.988   Durbin-Watson:                   1.252
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.225   Jarque-Bera (JB):                2.399
Skew:                           0.668   Prob(JB):                        0.301
Kurtosis:                       2.877   Cond. No.                         12.7
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

### VIF
vif = pd.DataFrame()

vif["Variable"] = X.columns
vif["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i)
              for i in range(X.shape[1])]

print(vif)

1       wt   1.766625

# MODEL 
## Simple Linear Regression
### Cell 3: Prepare the Variables
X = df[["Income"]]
X = sm.add_constant(X)

y = df["Consumption"]
In [86]:
### Cell 4: Fit the Model
model1 = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()
print(model1.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================

                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

==============================================================================

[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 5.05e+03. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.
In [87]:
### Cell 5: Heteroscedasticity Test
bp_test = het_breuschpagan(model1.resid, model1.model.exog)

print("LM Statistic :", bp_test[0])
print("LM p-value   :", bp_test[1])
print("F Statistic  :", bp_test[2])
print("F p-value    :", bp_test[3])

LM Statistic : 6.805057079710659
LM p-value   : 0.009090004781862582
F Statistic  : 7.925069123850486
F p-value    : 0.008053028131628304
In [88]:
### Cell 6: Normality Test
jb_test = jarque_bera(model1.resid)

print("JB Statistic :", jb_test[0])
print("p-value      :", jb_test[1])
print("Skewness     :", jb_test[2])
print("Kurtosis     :", jb_test[3])

JB Statistic : 3.1949075319392355
p-value      : 0.20241124881385675
Skewness     : -0.38315900493444455
Kurtosis     : 4.242052761510855
In [89]:
### Cell 7: Durbin-Watson Test
dw = durbin_watson(model1.resid)
print("Durbin-Watson Statistic =", dw)

Durbin-Watson Statistic = 0.8430634591987601
In [90]:
### Cell 8: Breusch-Godfrey Test
bg = acorr_breusch_godfrey(model1, nlags=1)

print("LM Statistic :", bg[0])
print("LM p-value   :", bg[1])
print("F Statistic  :", bg[2])
print("F p-value    :", bg[3])

LM Statistic : 10.468043862555517
LM p-value   : 0.0012145725820036156
F Statistic  : 13.529924836339157
F p-value    : 0.000830033530193847
In [91]:

# MODEL 3
## Log-Log Demand Model
### Cell 16: Take Logarithms
df2 = np.log(df2[["Quantity",
                           "Price",
                           "Income",
                           "SubPrice",
                           "CompPrice"]])

print(model3.summary())

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
In [102]:
### Cell 19: VIF
vif = pd.DataFrame()

vif["Variable"] = X.columns
vif["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i)
              for i in range(X.shape[1])]

print(vif)

    Variable          VIF
0      const  1508.830963
1      Price     1.022532
2     Income     1.019721
3   SubPrice     1.011602
4  CompPrice     1.009046
In [103]:
### Cell 20: Breusch-Pagan Test
bp = het_breuschpagan(model3.resid, model3.model.exog)

print("LM Statistic :", bp[0])
print("LM p-value   :", bp[1])
print("F Statistic  :", bp[2])
print("F p-value    :", bp[3])

LM Statistic : 4.940798291979432
LM p-value   : 0.29342607922282277
F Statistic  : 1.2344303058102657
F p-value    : 0.3015523872070202
In [104]:
### Cell 21: Jarque-Bera Test
jb = jarque_bera(model3.resid)

print("Statistic :", jb[0])
print("p-value   :", jb[1])
print("Skewness  :", jb[2])
print("Kurtosis  :", jb[3])

Statistic : 1.7442323969530866
p-value   : 0.4180659020505163
Skewness  : 0.2947348738024171
Kurtosis  : 2.7332769377660906

Notes for Student!!¶

  • Each cell performs one task only, making it easier to explain.
  • There are no custom functions, you can see every computation explicitly.
  • Variables such as X, y, model1, model2, and model3 are familiar and easy to follow.
  • Each diagnostic test is run independently, allowing you to explain the purpose and interpretation before moving to the next step.
  • you can execute the notebook cell by cell and observe the output after every step, which reinforces learning.
In [ ]:
